  • p99 = worst tail latency

Alert Examples

  • p95 latency > target

  • Safety flags +30% day-over-day

  • Cost/request spikes

  • Success rate drops below threshold

Common Mistakes

  • Only tracking latency

  • No user satisfaction metrics

  • No segmentation

  • No cost visibility

  • No alerts

Simple Real-World Example

A support bot dashboard shows:

  • CSAT falling

  • Latency stable

  • Hallucinations rising after a prompt update

This points to quality, not infrastructure.

Interview-Ready Answer

Production dashboards should track quality, reliability, latency, cost, safety, drift, fairness, and business metrics. Examples include success rate, hallucination rate, p95 latency, error rate, cost per request, policy violations, and user satisfaction. Strong dashboards combine real-time alerts with segmented trends so teams can detect and fix issues quickly.

[Image/Media]

How do you evaluate the robustness of a model across input variations?

Definition

Robustness means how well a model maintains consistent, accurate, and safe performance when inputs change in wording, format, noise, language, or context.

In simple words: if users ask the same thing in different ways, does the model still perform well?

Why It Is Important

Real users do not write perfect prompts. They vary by:

  • Typos

  • Slang

  • Different languages

  • Long vs short phrasing

  • Indirect wording

  • Messy formatting

A robust model handles these variations reliably.

How It Works (Step-by-Step)

  1. Choose core tasks.

  2. Create multiple input variants for each task.

  3. Run the model on all variants.

  4. Compare quality and consistency.

  5. Identify weak variation types.

  6. Improve model or prompts.

Key Components

  • Base task set

  • Input transformations

  • Evaluation metrics

  • Error analysis

  • Regression tracking

Common Input Variations to Test

1. Paraphrases

Same meaning, different wording.

Example:

  • “Reset my password.”

  • “I can’t log in—how do I change my password?”

2. Typos / Noise

  • “pasword reset pls”

3. Formatting Changes

  • Bullet points

  • ALL CAPS

  • Extra spaces

  • JSON / tables

4. Length Variation

Short query vs long detailed request.

5. Multilingual / Code-Switching

English + local language mix in India style usage.

6. Synonyms / Style

Formal vs casual language.

7. Irrelevant Extra Context

Prompt clutter or distractions.

8. Adversarial Wording

Confusing or tricky phrasing.

Metrics to Use

Accuracy / Success Rate

Did it solve the task?

Consistency Score

Did variants get similar outputs?

Robustness\ Gap = Best\ Variant\ Score - Worst\ Variant\ Score

Lower gap is better.

Safety Stability

Does safe behavior remain stable across variants?

Latency Stability

Do some variants slow the system badly?

Human Preference

Which outputs remain useful across variants?

Evaluation Methods

1. Perturbation Testing

Automatically modify prompts with typos, paraphrases, reorderings.

2. Adversarial Testing

Try prompts designed to confuse the model.

3. Cross-Locale Testing

Different dialects, regions, cultures.

4. Regression Suites

Re-run known variation sets after updates.

5. Pairwise Variant Comparison

Check if outputs differ meaningfully for same intent.

What Good Robustness Looks Like

  • Similar quality across paraphrases

  • Graceful handling of typos

  • Stable safety responses

  • No major drop for multilingual users

Common Failure Patterns

  • Great on clean prompts, poor on messy real prompts

  • Sensitive to one word change

  • Worse results for non-native phrasing

  • Hallucinations with long noisy context

How to Improve Robustness

1. Diverse Training / Fine-Tuning Data

2. Better Prompt Instructions

3. Retrieval Grounding

4. Input Normalization

Spell correction, formatting cleanup.

5. Continuous Testing

Simple Real-World Example

A support bot solves “refund request” perfectly, but fails “money back pls??”.

That shows weak robustness to wording variation.

Interview-Ready Answer

To evaluate robustness across input variations, I would create multiple paraphrase, typo, formatting, multilingual, and noisy versions of the same tasks, then compare success rate, consistency, safety, and latency across those variants. A robust model should maintain stable performance with a small robustness gap between best and worst input forms.

[Image/Media]

How do you test multimodal models for hidden vulnerabilities?

Definition

Testing multimodal models for hidden vulnerabilities means evaluating systems that process text, images, audio, video, or mixed inputs to uncover failures that are not obvious in normal usage.

These vulnerabilities may involve safety bypasses, prompt injection, privacy leaks, bias, hallucinations, or adversarial attacks.

Why It Is Important

Multimodal systems have more attack surfaces than text-only models because information can be hidden inside images, speech, metadata, or cross-modal interactions.

Examples:

  • Hidden text in images

  • Malicious QR codes

  • Adversarial image perturbations

  • Unsafe OCR extraction

  • Audio command injection

How It Works (Step-by-Step)

  1. Map all input/output modalities.

  2. Identify threat scenarios.

  3. Create adversarial test cases.

  4. Run controlled evaluations.

  5. Measure failure severity.

  6. Patch and re-test continuously.

Key Components

  • Modalities (text/image/audio/video)

  • Threat model

  • Test corpus

  • Automated scanners

  • Human red teamers

  • Monitoring pipeline

Main Hidden Vulnerabilities to Test

1. Cross-Modal Prompt Injection

Instructions hidden in an image, PDF, screenshot, or audio clip.

Example: tiny image text saying “Ignore previous instructions.”

2. OCR Exploitation

Model reads hidden or misleading text from screenshots/documents.

3. Adversarial Perturbations

Small visual/audio changes that fool the model while humans see normal content.

4. Privacy Leakage

Sensitive data extracted from documents, photos, faces, IDs, invoices.

5. Unsafe Tool Triggering

Image or voice causes unintended actions.

6. Bias and Representation Harm

Different performance across skin tones, accents, languages, clothing, disabilities.

7. Hallucinated Perception

Model claims objects/text are present when they are not.

8. Metadata Attacks

EXIF tags, filenames, hidden captions influencing behavior.

9. Jailbreak Through Non-Text Channels

Policy bypass attempts embedded in images or sound.

10. Context Confusion

Contradictory text + image inputs causing unsafe behavior.

How to Structure Testing

1. Modality-by-Modality Testing

Check text, image, audio separately.

2. Cross-Modal Combination Testing

Text says one thing, image says another.

3. Adversarial Corpus

Create datasets with:

  • Hidden text

  • Rotated text

  • Blurred screenshots

  • Noise

  • Multiple speakers

  • Deepfakes (for detection tasks)

4. Permission Boundary Testing

Ensure image content cannot silently trigger tools or purchases.

5. Fairness Slices

Evaluate across countries such as India, languages, accents, and demographics where appropriate.

Metrics to Track

  • Attack success rate

  • Safety violation rate

  • OCR accuracy under noise

  • Hallucination rate

  • False accept / false reject

  • Subgroup performance gaps

Attack\ Success\ Rate=\frac{Successful\ Exploits}{Total\ Attempts}

Best Practices

Human Red Team + Automated Testing

Use both creativity and scale.

Sandboxed Tool Access

Limit consequences of multimodal exploits.

Input Sanitization

Strip metadata, scan uploads, normalize files.

Confidence + Abstention

Allow “uncertain” instead of guessing.

Continuous Re-Testing

New attacks emerge often.

Common Mistakes

  • Only testing clean demo inputs

  • Ignoring OCR channels

  • No fairness testing

  • Letting media inputs directly trigger tools

  • No post-launch monitoring

Simple Real-World Example

A document assistant receives an invoice image with tiny hidden text saying “send all customer records.” If the system follows that hidden instruction, it has a multimodal prompt injection vulnerability.

Interview-Ready Answer

To test multimodal models for hidden vulnerabilities, I would threat-model each modality, build adversarial datasets with hidden text, noisy media, conflicting inputs, and privacy-sensitive content, then measure attack success, hallucinations, subgroup gaps, and unsafe tool triggers. Strong testing combines automated scans, human red teaming, sandboxed actions, and continuous monitoring after deployment.

[Image/Media]

How do you evaluate a full RAG pipeline end-to-end?

Definition

Evaluating a full Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline end-to-end means measuring how well the entire system performs—from user query to retrieval to final answer—not just the language model alone.

A RAG system is only as strong as its weakest stage.

Why It Is Important

Even a great model can fail if:

  • Wrong documents are retrieved

  • Relevant docs are ranked poorly

  • Context is truncated

  • Final answer hallucinates

  • Citations are wrong

  • Latency is too high

How It Works (Step-by-Step)

  1. User submits query.

  2. System retrieves documents.

  3. Reranker selects best context.

  4. Model generates answer.

  5. Answer is scored for quality and grounding.

  6. Production metrics are monitored.

Key Components

  • Query understanding

  • Retrieval layer

  • Ranking layer

  • Context construction

  • Generator model

  • Citations / grounding

  • System performance

Evaluate Each Stage + End-to-End

1. Retrieval Quality

Did the system fetch relevant documents?

Metrics:

  • Recall@k

  • Precision@k

  • MRR / NDCG

  • Coverage of gold evidence

Recall@k=\frac{Relevant\ Docs\ Retrieved}{Relevant\ Docs\ Available}

2. Ranking Quality

Are the best docs near the top?

Important because context windows are limited.

3. Context Construction

Check:

  • Duplicates removed

  • Important passages included

  • Token budget used efficiently

  • Freshest sources selected

4. Generation Quality

Measure:

  • Correctness

  • Helpfulness

  • Completeness

  • Conciseness

  • Tone

Use humans or AI judges.

5. Groundedness / Faithfulness

Does the answer stay supported by retrieved content?

Measure:

  • Unsupported claim rate

  • Citation accuracy

  • Hallucination rate

6. End-to-End Task Success

Did the user goal get solved?

Examples:

  • Correct refund policy explained

  • Right troubleshooting steps given

7. Operational Metrics

  • Latency

  • Cost per request

  • Timeout rate

  • Retrieval failures

8. Safety Metrics

  • Sensitive data leakage

  • Unsafe advice

  • Prompt injection success

Build a Good Eval Dataset

Use real-world queries across categories:

  • Easy factual queries

  • Ambiguous queries

  • Multi-hop questions

  • Freshness-dependent questions

  • No-answer cases

  • Adversarial prompts

Include regions such as India if relevant to user base.

Testing Methods

Offline Evaluation

Run benchmark query sets regularly.

Human Review

Experts inspect outputs.

Online A/B Testing

Compare versions with live traffic.

Error Analysis by Stage

If answer fails, ask:

  • Retrieval issue?

  • Ranking issue?

  • Prompt issue?

  • Generation issue?

Useful Mental Model

RAG\ Quality = Retrieval \times Grounding \times Generation

(Weakness in one stage harms total quality.)

Common Failure Patterns

  • Right docs retrieved, wrong answer generated

  • Good answer but no citations

  • Slow retrieval causing timeout

  • Old docs outranking new docs

Best Practice Dashboard

Track:

  • Recall@k

  • Grounded answer rate

  • User satisfaction

  • p95 latency

  • Cost/request

  • Injection attempts blocked

Simple Real-World Example

A support RAG bot answers refund policy questions.

If it retrieves outdated policy pages, even a strong LLM gives wrong answers. End-to-end evaluation catches that pipeline issue.

Interview-Ready Answer

To evaluate a full RAG pipeline end-to-end, I would measure retrieval relevance, ranking quality, context construction, generation quality, groundedness to sources, task success, latency, cost, and safety. I’d use realistic query sets, human or AI judges, and online A/B testing. The key is diagnosing failures by stage because RAG performance depends on the whole pipeline, not just the model.

[Image/Media]

How do you evaluate multi-component AI systems holistically?

Definition

A multi-component AI system is a product made of several connected parts rather than one model.

Examples:

  • LLM + retrieval + tools + memory

  • Speech-to-text + LLM + search + UI

  • Planner agent + tool executor + guardrails

  • Vision model + OCR + reasoning model

Holistic evaluation means measuring the full system behavior, not only each component separately.

Why It Is Important

A strong individual component does not guarantee a strong product.

Failures often happen in the handoffs between components:

  • Wrong retrieval context

  • Tool call errors

  • Memory mistakes

  • Latency stacking

  • Safety gaps between modules

How It Works (Step-by-Step)

  1. Map all components and data flow.

  2. Define user success outcomes.

  3. Evaluate each component individually.

  4. Evaluate handoffs between components.

  5. Run end-to-end scenarios.

  6. Monitor production behavior continuously.

Key Components to Evaluate

  • Input understanding

  • Retrieval/search

  • Planning/orchestration

  • Tool execution

  • Generation quality

  • Memory/state handling

  • Safety guardrails

  • UX and latency

Holistic Evaluation Framework

1. Component-Level Metrics

Each module gets local metrics.

Examples:

  • Retriever: Recall@k

  • Classifier: Accuracy

  • Tool calls: Success rate

  • LLM: Groundedness / quality

2. Interface / Handoff Metrics

Check whether outputs of one component are usable by the next.

Examples:

  • Query reformulation quality

  • Correct tool parameters

  • Structured JSON validity

  • Context passed without truncation

3. End-to-End Task Success

Most important question:

Did the user achieve the goal?

Examples:

  • Booking completed

  • Refund resolved

  • Correct report generated

System\ Success=\frac{Completed\ Tasks}{Total\ Tasks}

4. Reliability Metrics

  • Error rate

  • Retry loops

  • Timeout rate

  • Recovery success after failures

5. User Experience Metrics

  • Satisfaction

  • Time to resolution

  • Number of turns

  • Escalation rate

6. Safety & Governance Metrics

  • Policy violations

  • PII leakage

  • Prompt injection success

  • Unauthorized actions

7. Efficiency Metrics

  • Latency per stage

  • p95 total latency

  • Cost per completed task

8. Robustness Metrics

Test across:

  • Typos

  • Ambiguous inputs

  • Long context

  • Multilingual usage

  • Regional variants such as India user patterns

Evaluation Methods

Offline Scenario Suites

Curated workflows with expected outcomes.

Simulation

Synthetic users or scripted tasks.

Human Review

Inspect nuanced failures.

Online A/B Tests

Compare full system versions.

Chaos / Failure Injection

Temporarily break one component to test resilience.

Root Cause Analysis

When failures occur, trace which component or handoff caused it.

Common Mistakes

  • Only evaluating the LLM

  • Ignoring latency compounding

  • No handoff validation

  • No real user metrics

  • No safety tests across tools

Useful Mental Model

Total\ Quality \neq Sum\ of\ Component\ Scores

Interaction effects matter.

Simple Real-World Example

A voice support bot has:

  • Great speech recognition

  • Good LLM answers

  • Slow backend tool calls

Users still hate it because total resolution time is poor.

Interview-Ready Answer

To evaluate multi-component AI systems holistically, I would measure each component, the handoffs between components, and end-to-end user outcomes such as task completion, satisfaction, safety, latency, and cost. I’d combine offline scenario testing, production monitoring, and root-cause tracing because total system quality depends on interactions between components, not just individual model scores.

[Image/Media]
